audits.title: Layout shift culprits
audits.description: Layout shifts occur when elements move absent any user interaction., such as elements being added, removed, or their fonts changing as the page loads.

audits.title: Image elements do not have explicit `width` and `height`
audits.description: Set an explicit width and height on image elements to reduce layout shifts and improve CLS.

audits.title: Reduce unused CSS
audits.description: Reduce unused rules from stylesheets and defer CSS not used for above-the-fold content to decrease bytes consumed by network activity..

audits.title: Render-blocking requests
audits.description: Requests are blocking the page's initial render, which may delay LCP. can move these network requests out of the critical path.

audits.title: `[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents
audits.description: Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers..

audits.title: Links do not have a discernible name
audits.description: Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users..

audits.title: Largest Contentful Paint
audits.description: Largest Contentful Paint marks the time at which the largest text or image is painted.

audits.title: Avoid multiple page redirects
audits.description: Redirects introduce additional delays before the page can be loaded..

audits.title: `[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5.
audits.description: Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page..

audits.title: Use efficient cache lifetimes
audits.description: A long cache lifetime can speed up repeat visits to your page..

audits.title: Network dependency tree
audits.description: by reducing the length of chains, reducing the download size of resources, or deferring the download of unnecessary resources to improve page load.

audits.title: Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio.
audits.description: Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read..

audits.title: Document request latency
audits.description: Your first network request is the most important. by avoiding redirects, ensuring a fast server response, and enabling text compression.

audits.title: Font display
audits.description: Consider setting to swap or optional to ensure text is consistently visible. swap can be further optimized to mitigate layout shifts with.

audits.title: Legacy JavaScript
audits.description: Polyfills and transforms enable older browsers to use new JavaScript features. However, many aren't necessary for modern browsers. Consider modifying your JavaScript build process to not transpile features, unless you know you must support older browsers.

audits.title: Max Potential First Input Delay
audits.description: The maximum potential First Input Delay that your users could experience is the duration of the longest task..

audits.title: LCP breakdown
audits.description: Each. Ideally, most of the LCP time should be spent on loading the resources, not within delays.

audits.title: Forced reflow
audits.description: A forced reflow occurs when JavaScript queries geometric properties (such as offsetWidth) after styles have been invalidated by a change to the DOM state. This can result in poor performance. Learn more about and possible mitigations.

audits.title: Links are not crawlable
audits.description: Search engines may use `href` attributes on links to crawl websites. Ensure that the `href` attribute of anchor elements links to an appropriate destination, so more pages of the site can be discovered.

audits.title: Reduce JavaScript execution time
audits.description: Consider reducing the time spent parsing, compiling, and executing JS. You may find delivering smaller JS payloads helps with this..

audits.title: Reduce unused JavaScript
audits.description: Reduce unused JavaScript and defer loading scripts until they are required to decrease bytes consumed by network activity..

audits.title: Select elements do not have associated label elements.
audits.description: Form elements without effective labels can create frustrating experiences for screen reader users..

audits.title: Cumulative Layout Shift
audits.description: Cumulative Layout Shift measures the movement of visible elements within the viewport..

audits.title: Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0
audits.description: A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies..

terms.da: Domain Authority: a 0 to 100 trust score based on the quality of sites linking to you. The higher it is, the easier everything else ranks.
terms.pa: Page Authority: the same trust idea as Domain Authority but for one specific page rather than the whole site.
terms.cls: Cumulative Layout Shift: how much your page jumps around while loading. Jumpy pages frustrate users and rank worse.
terms.csp: Content-Security-Policy: a security header that blocks malicious scripts. Security reviews check for it.
terms.geo: Generative Engine Optimisation: whether AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews) actually name and cite your business when a buyer asks them for a provider like you.
terms.lcp: Largest Contentful Paint: how long your main content takes to appear. Over 2.5 seconds and Google ranks you lower and visitors leave.
terms.nap: Name, Address, Phone. Search engines cross-check these to trust that your business is real and local. Inconsistent or missing NAP sinks local rankings.
terms.ccpa: California's privacy law, and the template for the roughly 20 US states that now have one. It gives consumers the right to opt out and to have data deleted.
terms.gdpr: The data-protection law for the UK and EU. Fines reach GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover, so gaps here are the most expensive on the page.
terms.hsts: A security header that stops your site being downgraded from secure HTTPS. Corporate buyers check for it, so its absence is an easy red flag.
terms.pecr: UK rules on cookies and electronic marketing. You must get clear consent before non-essential cookies or trackers fire.
terms.sameas: Schema links that connect your site to your verified profiles (LinkedIn, Companies House, Wikidata) so AI knows the business is really you.
terms.schema: Hidden code (structured data) that tells search engines and AI exactly who you are, what you offer and where. Without it they have to guess, and they guess wrong.
terms.e-e-a-t: Google's test of Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness and Trust. It decides whether your content deserves to rank, and it matters most in law, health and finance.
terms.organic: The unpaid search results, as opposed to ads. Ranking here is the durable, compounding kind of visibility.
terms.uk gdpr: The UK version of the EU data-protection law, enforced by the ICO with fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover.
terms.llms.txt: A simple file that tells AI models what your site is about and what they may use. It is a new standard and most firms do not have one yet, so adding it is an easy edge.
terms.map pack: The three-business map result at the top of local searches. The top spots take the majority of local clicks.
terms.wikidata: Google's and AI's open knowledge base. An entry there makes you a recognised entity that answer engines can confidently cite.
terms.canonical: A line of code that tells Google which version of a page is the real one, so duplicate web addresses do not split your ranking power.
terms.ai crawler: The automated readers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, Google-Extended) that AI answer engines send to read your site. Block them and those engines cannot cite you.
terms.local pack: The three businesses Google shows on a small map at the top of local searches. Most local clicks go to those three, so being outside it costs real enquiries.
terms.robots.txt: A file that tells web crawlers which parts of your site they may read. One wrong line here can make you invisible to Google or to AI engines.
terms.ai visibility: Whether the AI answer engines buyers now ask first will name and cite you, or only your competitors.
terms.share of voice: How often you appear versus your competitors when buyers search or ask AI in your category. Low share of voice means they are heard and you are not.
terms.structured data: Hidden code that spells out who you are and what you offer so Google and AI can read it precisely instead of guessing.
terms.domain authority: A 0 to 100 score of how much trust your site has earned from links on other websites. Higher means Google and AI engines are more likely to rank and cite you. It is the single biggest reason one site outranks another.
terms.meta description: The summary Google shows under your result. A good one lifts click-through; if it is missing, Google writes its own from random page text.

regimes.why: applies to every site that wants to rank in Google or be cited by AI answer engines, regardless of where it is registered

jurisdiction_statement.statement: harleystreetdentalclinic is registered in the United Kingdom and its own website shows it serves clients in the UK. It is therefore bound by UK law (UK GDPR, PECR, CMA/DMCC, sector regulators), not only the law of its country of registration. This audit applies the laws of every jurisdiction the site shows you operate in, and no others.
